summary: Joel Spolsky walks through the economics of software pricing, starting with textbook demand curves and profit maximization, then systematically demolishes each theoretical assumption. He explains how market segmentation can capture consumer surplus but warns it breeds customer resentment, how the software market naturally splits into three price tiers (free, cheap, and dear) with a dead zone between $1,000 and $75,000, and why focus groups are useless for determining willingness to pay. He argues that low pricing can be a strategic investment in grassroots adoption and word-of-mouth growth, using FogBugz's success versus expensive competitors like Rational as evidence. The post concludes by admitting that pricing remains fundamentally unsolvable because demand curves aren't reliably measurable and prices themselves function as quality signals.
key_points: Market segmentation captures consumer surplus but erodes customer goodwill — airlines perfected segmentation and customers fled to low-cost carriers the moment they could

Software pricing falls into three natural tiers: free, cheap ($10-$1,000), and dear ($75,000-$1,000,000), with a dead zone in between caused by corporate purchasing overhead that inflates the cost of each sale to ~$50,000
Low pricing is an investment in future growth: cheap software gets adopted by individuals who evangelize it, while expensive software sits on shelves after being force-purchased by executives
Demand curves can't be reliably measured because focus groups lie, results vary wildly day to day, and prices themselves act as quality signals that distort the curve
Sophisticated corporate purchasers will reverse-segment you by exploiting sales commission structures and quarterly quotas to extract the lowest possible price
money_quotes: The biggest mistake software companies make is charging too little, so they don't get enough income, and they have to go out of business. An even bigger mistake, yes, even bigger than the biggest mistake, is charging too much, so they don't get enough customers, and they have to go out of business.
There's no software priced between $1000 and $75,000. The minute you charge more than $1000 you need to get serious corporate signoffs.

Big companies protect themselves so well against the risk of buying something expensive that they actually drive up the cost of the expensive stuff, from $1000 to $75000, which mostly goes towards the cost of jumping all the hurdles that they set up to insure that no purchase can possibly go wrong.
Nobody comes out of college and starts a dotcom using BEA technology, because they couldn't afford BEA technology in college.

Corporations have full time purchasing departments staffed with people whose entire job is whittling down prices. These people go to conferences where they learn how to get the best price. They practice saying 'no. cheaper.' all day long in front of mirrors.
People would rather pay $100 when everyone else is paying $100 than pay $79 if they know there's someone out there who got it for $78.

When you're setting a price, you're sending a signal. If your competitor's software ranges in price from about $100 to about $500, and you decide, heck, my product is about in the middle of the road, so I'll sell it for $300, well, what message do you think you're sending to your customers? You're telling them that you think your software is 'eh.'
The truth is, the only way to determine how much someone will pay for something is to put it up for sale, and see how many people actually buy it.
key_insight: Software pricing theory is elegant but useless in practice because demand curves can't be measured, segmentation breeds resentment, prices function as quality signals, and the market naturally collapses into just three viable price points with a dead zone in between.
